note: Ground truth is committed before any paired run and kept outside every standalone fixture copy presented to the auditor.

corrections.trigger: The first blinded control observation cited the limiter registration line and identified an unintended missing token-consumption control.
corrections.change: Accept either side of the mount-order evidence, record the second real violation, exclude case-01 from lift, and add pre-run replacement case-07.
corrections.reason: Calling the valid limiter citation a miss or the missing single-use transition a false positive would make the grader wrong. Because the correction followed a model observation, case-01 cannot support causal lift.

corrections.trigger: The completed paired matrix repeatedly identified valid A-SEC-6 violations outside the intended seed in four fixtures.
corrections.change: Record missing token consumption in cases 02-05 and ordinary credential equality in case-03 as post-run, unscored true findings. Classify repeated citations to one expected defect as duplicates rather than false positives.
corrections.reason: The source and check text independently confirm these violations. Counting them as false positives would punish correct findings; counting them as pre-authored hits would introduce hindsight bias. They remain visible but do not contribute to causal lift.

cases.claim: Excluded diagnostic fixture with two real violations discovered during the first control observation.

findings.claim: The password-reset route is registered before the limiter that is intended to protect password routes.
Successful reset-token verification does not mark the token used, so it remains reusable until expiration.

cases.claim: The intended seed is ordinary reset-token equality; missing token consumption was discovered after the runs.

findings.claim: A reset-token digest is compared with ordinary string equality instead of a constant-time comparison.
Successful reset-token verification does not mark the token used, so it remains reusable until expiration.

cases.claim: The intended seed is fast password hashing; ordinary credential equality and missing token consumption were discovered after the runs.

findings.claim: Passwords are stored with one fast SHA-256 digest rather than a supported password KDF.
Password digests are compared with ordinary string equality rather than a constant-time comparison.
Successful reset-token verification does not mark the token used, so it remains reusable until expiration.

cases.claim: The intended seed is non-cryptographic token generation; missing token consumption was discovered after the runs.

findings.claim: Reset tokens are generated with Math.random rather than a cryptographically secure generator.
Successful reset-token verification does not mark the token used, so it remains reusable until expiration.

cases.claim: The intended seed is a development bypass; missing token consumption was discovered after the runs.

findings.claim: A development environment branch bypasses password verification.
Successful reset-token verification does not mark the token used, so it remains reusable until expiration.

cases.claim: The control uses bcrypt, constant-time reset-token comparison, CSPRNG single-use tokens, generic errors, and limiter-before-route mounting.
The password-reset route is registered before the limiter that is intended to protect password routes; all other A-SEC-6 controls are present.
